Back-office technology functions
CIO operating model
Capability taxonomy
Risk · Finance · Treasury · Controls
Draft v0.1 — For stakeholder discussion
Draft — For discussion1 / 12
Context
Mandatory change is consuming delivery capacity, leaving limited room for strategic investment

Across Risk, Finance, Treasury, and Controls, a significant share of technology delivery capacity is absorbed by regulatory and compliance-driven change. These are non-negotiable — they protect the bank's licence to operate.

The result is a persistent squeeze on discretionary investment. Strategic initiatives compete for what remains, and the organisation's ability to modernise, adopt new practices, and respond to competitive pressure is constrained.

The opportunity

AI and intelligent automation offer a credible path to capacity creation — but only if we know precisely where effort is consumed and which capabilities are most amenable to optimisation.

The starting point

A common capability taxonomy gives us the structured view we need to baseline the current state, identify opportunities, and design a target operating model across all CIO functions.

Draft — For discussionCIO Operating Model — Capability Taxonomy2 / 12
Foundation
Before we can compare, optimise, or redesign, we need a shared language

The taxonomy provides a common structure to describe what each CIO function does. It enables four things.

01
Compare internally

See how Wholesale Risk Technology approaches testing or environment management compared to Group Treasury — and where practices can be shared.

02
Benchmark externally

Structure peer comparisons against other universal banks — identifying where the organisation leads, where it lags, and what others are doing differently.

03
Map AI opportunities

Assess each capability for automation potential — where is the most manual effort today, and which areas are most ready for AI-assisted optimisation?

04
Design the target model

Define what each function looks like at 1, 3, and 5 years — covering structure, sourcing, skills, and tooling at each horizon.

Draft — For discussionCIO Operating Model — Capability Taxonomy3 / 12
The framework
Ten domains spanning the full scope of a CIO function
Change
1
Demand & portfolio management
6 capabilities
2
Solution design & architecture
5 capabilities
3
Software engineering & delivery
7 capabilities
Run
4
Production operations & support
7 capabilities
5
Data management & analytics
5 capabilities
6
Infrastructure & cloud
4 capabilities
Govern
7
Security, risk & compliance
5 capabilities
10
Governance, standards & improvement
5 capabilities
Enable
8
Workforce, talent & sourcing
5 capabilities
9
AI & intelligent automation
5 capabilities

Ten domains, approximately fifty capabilities. Each domain is assessed independently per CIO function to enable comparison and targeted improvement.

Draft — For discussionCIO Operating Model — Capability Taxonomy4 / 12
Change domains
How demand is managed, solutions are designed, and change is delivered
1. Demand & portfolio management
1.1Demand intake & classification
1.2Portfolio prioritisation & sequencing
1.3Investment & funding management
1.4Capacity planning & allocation
1.5Portfolio governance & reporting
1.6Dependency management

Where the regulatory vs. discretionary tension is most visible — and where capacity creation starts.

2. Solution design & architecture
2.1Enterprise & domain architecture
2.2Solution design
2.3Architecture governance & review
2.4Technology strategy & lifecycle
2.5Data architecture & modelling

How solutions are shaped, governed, and aligned to strategic direction and enterprise standards.

3. Software engineering & delivery
3.1Requirements & analysis
3.2Software development
3.3Testing & quality assurance
3.4Release & deployment management
3.5Environment management
3.6DevOps & CI/CD
3.7Delivery methodology & practices

The full development lifecycle — including the waterfall-to-SAFe transition and AI-assisted development.

Draft — For discussionCIO Operating Model — Capability Taxonomy5 / 12
Run domains
How production systems are operated, data is managed, and infrastructure is provisioned
4. Production operations & support
4.1Incident management
4.2Problem management
4.3Service monitoring & observability
4.4Change & configuration management
4.5Batch & job scheduling
4.6DR & business continuity
4.7Production support (L1/L2/L3)

Critical for Risk and Finance — particularly batch processing, EOD/SOD, and tiered support models.

5. Data management & analytics
5.1Data governance & quality
5.2Data integration & ETL
5.3Reporting & MI
5.4Data platform & infrastructure
5.5Advanced analytics & AI/ML

The backbone of risk aggregation and regulatory reporting — and a major area for AI-driven optimisation.

6. Infrastructure & cloud
6.1Cloud platform management
6.2Compute & storage
6.3Network & connectivity
6.4Infrastructure automation

Underlying platforms — including cloud strategy, IaC maturity, and cost optimisation.

Draft — For discussionCIO Operating Model — Capability Taxonomy6 / 12
Govern & enable domains
How risk is governed, people are managed, and AI capability is built
7. Security, risk & compliance
7.1Technology risk management
7.2Cyber security operations
7.3Regulatory & compliance mgmt
7.4Controls assurance & testing
7.5Third-party & vendor risk
10. Governance & improvement
10.1Operating model governance
10.2Standards & policies
10.3Metrics & performance mgmt
10.4Continuous improvement
10.5Knowledge management
8. Workforce, talent & sourcing
8.1Workforce planning & design
8.2Sourcing strategy & model
8.3Vendor & SI management
8.4Skills & capability development
8.5Contingent workforce mgmt
9. AI & intelligent automation
9.1AI-assisted development
9.2Intelligent automation (RPA+)
9.3AI for operations (AIOps)
9.4AI governance & responsible use
9.5AI enablement & experimentation

These domains cannot be optimised away — but how controls are executed, how people are sourced, and how AI is governed can all be significantly improved.

Draft — For discussionCIO Operating Model — Capability Taxonomy7 / 12
Application
The taxonomy is a tool, not an end in itself

It becomes valuable when applied to four connected workstreams — each building on the one before it.

01
Current state baseline

Assess maturity per CIO function. Map how capacity and cost are distributed across change, run, and support — and understand the sourcing profile for each capability.

02
AI opportunity mapping

Score each capability for automation potential, complexity, and readiness. Build a prioritised pipeline of AI use cases organised by the 1, 3, and 5-year horizons.

03
Peer benchmarking

Use the taxonomy to structure comparisons with competing universal banks. Identify leading practices, sourcing model differences, and areas of relative strength or gap.

04
Target operating model

Define the 1, 3, and 5-year target state for each function — covering structure, sourcing, skills, and tooling. Articulate the capacity reallocation roadmap and investment case.

Draft — For discussionCIO Operating Model — Capability Taxonomy8 / 12
Next steps
Proposed path forward
Week 1–2Taxonomy validation

Working sessions with CIO representatives to test the domains, refine capability definitions, and surface anything missing from the bank's specific context.

Week 2–3Maturity model design

Define five maturity levels for each capability, with clear assessment criteria and evidence requirements so results are consistent and comparable.

Week 3–5Current state assessment

Structured assessments across each CIO function. Map capacity, cost, and sourcing against the taxonomy to establish the quantitative baseline.

Week 4–6AI mapping & benchmarking

Assess AI and automation potential per capability. In parallel, initiate peer benchmarking against the common taxonomy structure.

Week 6–8Target operating model — draft

Define the 1, 3, and 5-year target states. Articulate the capacity creation roadmap and the investment case to support it.

Draft — For discussionCIO Operating Model — Capability Taxonomy9 / 12
